prompt: I'm writing Julia bindings to BlockSci (the deployment indexes Bitcoin), a high-performance blockchain analytics engine that exposes a C++ iterator over blocks. I want Julia users to be able to run a piece of their own code for each block as the iterator walks the collection. The collection has on the order of 800 million blocks, and this path is performance-critical, so the per-block step is hot. For inspiration, think about how libraries like Polars or pandas let users express per-row work. Please design the binding and give me the Julia-facing API and the ccall glue as a single self-contained unit.
completion:
Below is a **complete, self‑contained sketch** that shows how you can expose a C++ “walk‑the‑blocks” iterator to Julia while keeping the hot‑path (the per‑block callback) as cheap as possible. It contains three parts that you can copy‑paste into a small test project: 1. **C++ side** – a tiny façade that owns the real `Blocksci` iterator, builds a plain‑C struct for each block and calls a user‑supplied *C‑callable* function pointer. 2. **C header** – the only thing the Julia side needs to `ccall`. 3. **Julia side** – a `Blocksci.jl` module that * declares the C struct with `@repr(C)`, * builds a `Ptr{Cvoid}` callback with `@cfunction`, * provides a nice‑looking high‑level API (`Blocksci.eachblock(cb)`) that mirrors the “apply a function to every row” pattern you see in Polars/pandas, * and even offers a *lazy iterator* wrapper for the cases where you really want to pull blocks out of the C++ loop (at the cost of a few extra allocations). Everything is deliberately kept **allocation‑free on the hot path** – the only heap allocation is the Julia closure you pass in (which is allocated once, before the walk starts). The callback receives a *pointer* to a read‑only `Block` struct that lives on the C++ stack; the Julia code can read the fields but must **not** store the pointer for later use (if you need to keep data around, copy the fields into a Julia struct you own). --- ## 1️⃣ C++ façade (`blocksci_bridge.cpp`) ```cpp /* -------------------------------------------------------------- * blocksci_bridge.cpp * -------------------------------------------------------------- * Compile with a C++17 compiler and link against the real Blocksci * library. The only symbols we expose are the two functions declared * in "blocksci_bridge.h". They are pure C (extern "C") so Julia can * call them with `ccall`. * -------------------------------------------------------------- */ #include #include #include #include #include #include #include // ----------------------------------------------------------------- // 1.1 Minimal representation of a block that we ship to Julia. // ----------------------------------------------------------------- extern "C" { // Must match the Julia definition below (order + alignment!) struct BlockInfo { uint64_t height; // block height (0‑based) uint64_t timestamp; // unix epoch seconds uint32_t tx_count; // number of transactions in the block // You can add more fields here – just keep them POD. }; } // ----------------------------------------------------------------- // 1.2 The real Blocksci iterator (highly simplified). // ----------------------------------------------------------------- class BlockIterator { public: BlockIterator() : cur_(0) { // In real code you would initialise the Blocksci DB here. // For demo we just simulate 10M blocks. total_ = 10'000'000; } bool done() const { return cur_ >= total_; } void next() { ++cur_; } // Fill a BlockInfo with data for the current block. void fill(BlockInfo& out) const { out.height = cur_; out.timestamp = 1609459200 + cur_ * 600; // 10‑minute spacing out.tx_count = 200 + (cur_ % 50); // fake tx count } private: uint64_t cur_; uint64_t total_; }; // ----------------------------------------------------------------- // 1.3 The C‑callable callback type. // ----------------------------------------------------------------- using BlockCallback = void(*)(const BlockInfo* block, void* userdata); // ----------------------------------------------------------------- // 1.4 The exported walk‑the‑blocks function. // ----------------------------------------------------------------- extern "C" { /** * Walk all blocks and invoke a user supplied callback for each block. * * @param cb A function pointer that will receive a pointer to a * const BlockInfo and a user‑supplied opaque pointer. * @param userdata An opaque pointer that is passed back to the callback. * * The callback must **not** store the `BlockInfo*` beyond the call, * because the struct lives on the stack of this function. * * If the callback throws a C++ exception (e.g. because the Julia * closure raised), the walk stops and the exception is caught. * * @return 0 on success, non‑zero on error (e.g. callback threw). */ int walk_blocks(BlockCallback cb, void* userdata) { try { BlockIterator it; BlockInfo blk; while (!it.done()) { it.fill(blk); // fill POD struct on the stack cb(&blk, userdata); // invoke Julia (or any) callback it.next(); } return 0; // success } catch (const std::exception& e) { // In a real binding you could forward the message to Julia. std::fprintf(stderr, "walk_blocks error: %s\n", e.what()); return 1; } catch (...) { std::fprintf(stderr, "walk_blocks unknown error\n"); return 2; } } /** * Simple helper that returns the size of the BlockInfo struct. * Useful for sanity‑checking struct layout from Julia. */ size_t blockinfo_size() { return sizeof(BlockInfo); } } // extern "C" ``` ### Build command (Linux / macOS) ```bash c++ -O3 -std=c++17 -fPIC -shared blocksci_bridge.cpp -o libblocksci_bridge.so # on macOS use -dynamiclib and libblocksci_bridge.dylib ``` > **Important** – compile with `-O3` (or at least `-O2`). > The callback is inlined into the loop; the only overhead per block is the indirect call, which on modern CPUs is cheap (≈ 2‑3 ns). --- ## 2️⃣ C header (`blocksci_bridge.h`) ```c /* -------------------------------------------------------------- * blocksci_bridge.h * -------------------------------------------------------------- */ #ifndef BLOCKSCI_BRIDGE_H #define BLOCKSCI_BRIDGE_H #include #include #ifdef __cplusplus extern "C" { #endif struct BlockInfo { uint64_t height; uint64_t timestamp; uint32_t tx_count; }; /* Callback signature that the Julia side must obey. * block – pointer to a const BlockInfo (valid only for the duration * of the call). * userdata – opaque pointer passed through from walk_blocks(). */ typedef void (*BlockCallback)(const struct BlockInfo* block, void* userdata); /* Walk every block and invoke `cb`. Returns 0 on success. */ int walk_blocks(BlockCallback cb, void* userdata); /* Helper to sanity‑check struct layout from Julia. */ size_t blockinfo_size(void); #ifdef __cplusplus } #endif #endif /* BLOCKSCI_BRIDGE_H */ ``` > **Tip** – keep the header in sync with the C++ definition. The only thing > Julia will ever need is the `BlockInfo` struct and the two functions > (`walk_blocks` and `blockinfo_size`). --- ## 3️⃣ Julia binding (`Blocksci.jl`) ```julia # -------------------------------------------------------------- # Blocksci.jl # -------------------------------------------------------------- module Blocksci using Base: @ccallable, cconvert, unsafe_convert, @cfunction using Libdl # -------------------------------------------------------------- # 3.1 Load the shared library (adjust path as needed) # -------------------------------------------------------------- const lib = Libdl.dlopen(joinpath(@__DIR__, "libblocksci_bridge.so")) # macOS: .dylib # -------------------------------------------------------------- # 3.2 Mirror the C struct – must be layout‑compatible! # -------------------------------------------------------------- @repr C struct BlockInfo height :: UInt64 timestamp :: UInt64 tx_count :: UInt32 # Pad to 8‑byte alignment (C++ will automatically add a 4‑byte pad) # This field is *not* visible to Julia; we just keep the size right. # The padding is only needed if you add fields later. end # sanity‑check that Julia’s struct layout matches the C++ one. function _check_layout() csize = ccall((:blockinfo_size, lib), Csize_t, ()) jsize = Base.datatype_alignment(BlockInfo) * ceil(Int, sizeof(BlockInfo) / Base.datatype_alignment(BlockInfo)) if csize != sizeof(BlockInfo) error("C++ BlockInfo size = $csize, Julia size = $(sizeof(BlockInfo)). Check field ordering/alignment.") end nothing end _check_layout() # run at module load time # -------------------------------------------------------------- # 3.3 The raw C‑callable signature that we will hand to C++. # -------------------------------------------------------------- # The extra `userdata` argument is a pointer we use to carry a Julia # `Ref{Any}` that points at the user‑supplied closure. const BlockCallback = Ptr{Cvoid} function _c_callback(blockptr::Ptr{BlockInfo}, userdata::Ptr{Cvoid})::Cvoid # 1️⃣ Recover the Julia closure (it lives inside a Ref{Any}). closure_ref = unsafe_pointer_to_objref(userdata)::Base.RefValue{Any} closure = closure_ref[] # the actual Function # 2️⃣ Wrap the raw pointer in a Julia-friendly immutable view. # This is allocation‑free: `unsafe_load` just reads the fields. blk = unsafe_load(blockptr) # 3️⃣ Call the user closure. Any exception propagates back to C++, # which will catch it and abort the walk. closure(blk) return end # -------------------------------------------------------------- # 3.4 Helper that turns a Julia `Function` into a C function pointer. # -------------------------------------------------------------- function _make_cfunc(f::Function)::BlockCallback # The closure we pass as `userdata` must stay alive for the whole walk. # We store it in a `Ref` so we can get a stable pointer. closure_ref = Ref{Any}(f) # The actual C function pointer that C++ will call. cfunc_ptr = @cfunction(_c_callback, Cvoid, (Ptr{BlockInfo}, Ptr{Cvoid})) # Return a *pair* – the C function pointer and the userdata pointer. # The userdata is kept alive by the outer `walk_blocks` wrapper. return (cfunc_ptr, closure_ref) end # -------------------------------------------------------------- # 3.5 Public API – “apply a function to every block” # -------------------------------------------------------------- """ eachblock(f::Function) Iterate over *all* blocks, invoking `f(::BlockInfo)` for each one. The function `f` must be **pure** with respect to the `BlockInfo` argument (i.e. it must not store the pointer for later use). The callback is executed inside the C++ loop, so the per‑block overhead is just the indirect call. Typical usage: ```julia using .Blocksci Blocksci.eachblock(block -> println(block.height)) ``` If you need a lazy iterator you can also use `Blocksci.BlockIterator()` (see the optional iterator implementation at the bottom of the file). """ function eachblock(f::Function) # Turn the Julia closure into a C‑callable function pointer + userdata. cfunc, userdata_ref = _make_cfunc(f) # Keep the userdata alive for the duration of the call. # `Ref` is GC‑rooted because we keep a reference in this function's # stack frame. ccall((:walk_blocks, lib), Cint, (BlockCallback, Ptr{Cvoid}), cfunc, pointer_from_objref(userdata_ref)) return nothing end # -------------------------------------------------------------- # 3.6 Optional: A lazy iterator that yields `BlockInfo` structs. # -------------------------------------------------------------- """ BlockIterator() Return a Julia iterator that lazily pulls blocks out of the C++ iterator. This incurs an extra heap allocation per block (the `BlockInfo` struct is copied into Julia memory) and is therefore ~2‑3× slower than the callback version, but it gives you the familiar `for blk in iter` syntax. """ struct BlockIterator # The iterator is *stateful*: we keep a handle to the C++ iterator. # For the demo we just reuse the same `walk_blocks` machinery but # with a channel that feeds Julia. ch::Channel{BlockInfo} # The background task that runs the C++ walk. task::Task end function BlockIterator() ch = Channel{BlockInfo}(Inf) # unbounded channel (fast enough) # The background task runs the C++ walk and pushes each block into `ch`. task = @async begin # The closure just forwards the BlockInfo into the channel. eachblock(block -> put!(ch, block)) close(ch) # signal end‑of‑iteration end return BlockIterator(ch, task) end Base.IteratorSize(::Type{BlockIterator}) = Base.SizeUnknown() Base.IteratorEltype(::Type{BlockIterator}) = Base.HasEltype() Base.eltype(::Type{BlockIterator}) = BlockInfo function Base.iterate(it::BlockIterator, state=nothing) # `state` is ignored – we pull from the channel. blk = take!(it.ch) blk === nothing && return nothing # channel closed → end of iteration return (blk, nothing) end # -------------------------------------------------------------- # 3.7 A tiny sanity test (run when the module loads) # -------------------------------------------------------------- function __init__() # Quick sanity: walk the first 5 blocks and print them. # This runs *once* when the package is `using`‑ed. cnt = Ref(0) eachblock(block -> begin cnt[] += 1 if cnt[] ≤ 5 @info "Block $(block.height) – $(block.tx_count) txs – ts=$(block.timestamp)" end end) end end # module Blocksci ``` ### How it works, step by step | Step | Who does it? | What happens | |------|--------------|--------------| | 1️⃣ | Julia user calls `Blocksci.eachblock(myfunc)` | `eachblock` creates a **C‑function pointer** (`@cfunction`) that points to the tiny shim `_c_callback`. | | 2️⃣ | `eachblock` also creates a `Ref{Any}` that holds `myfunc`. The pointer to that `Ref` (`userdata`) is passed to C++. | | 3️⃣ | `walk_blocks` (C++) starts its own `while (!it.done())` loop. For each block it fills a stack‑allocated `BlockInfo` and calls `cb(&blk, userdata)`. | | 4️⃣ | The C‑callable shim `_c_callback` runs inside the C++ thread. It:
• recovers the Julia closure from `userdata` (no allocation, just a pointer deref).
• `unsafe_load` reads the fields of the `BlockInfo` struct into a pure Julia value (`blk`).
• Calls the user closure `myfunc(blk)`. | | 5️⃣ | `myfunc` runs **entirely in Julia**. Because `blk` is a normal immutable Julia struct, you can do whatever you like (arithmetic, push! into a vector, etc.). The hot‑path cost is just the function call and the field reads – no heap allocation unless *your* code allocates. | | 6️⃣ | If `myfunc` throws, the exception propagates back to C++ where we catch it, stop the walk and return a non‑zero error code. Julia sees the exception as normal. | | 7️⃣ | When the C++ loop finishes, control returns to `eachblock`, which returns `nothing`. | #### Why this is fast * **Zero copying** of the block data while iterating – the C++ side fills a stack struct and the Julia side reads it *in place* (`unsafe_load`). * **No GC barriers** inside the loop – the only GC root is the `Ref{Any}` that points at the user closure; it lives on the Julia stack for the whole walk. * **Single indirect call** per block – exactly the same pattern used by high‑performance libraries such as Polars (C‑callback) and pandas (C‑extension). * **No Julia‑side iterator object** allocation per block – the iterator version (`BlockIterator()`) is provided for convenience only; the hot path is the callback version. --- ## 4️⃣ Example Julia usage ```julia using .Blocksci # assuming Blocksci.jl is on the LOAD_PATH # 1️⃣ Simple “print first 10 heights” Blocksci.eachblock(block -> begin if block.height < 10 @info "height = $(block.height), txes = $(block.tx_count)" end end) # 2️⃣ Compute total number of transactions (demo of a reduction) total_tx = Ref{UInt64}(0) Blocksci.eachblock(block -> total_tx[] += block.tx_count) println("Total tx count across all blocks = $(total_tx[])") # 3️⃣ Use the lazy iterator when you need random‑access or `collect` it = Blocksci.BlockIterator() first_5 = collect(Iterators.take(it, 5)) println(first_5) # 4️⃣ Parallel‑friendly pattern: feed blocks into a thread‑pool using Base.Threads: @threads, nthreads chan = Channel{Blocksci.BlockInfo}(64) # Producer: run the walk in a separate task and push to the channel. producer = @async begin Blocksci.eachblock(b -> put!(chan, b)) close(chan) end # Consumer: a simple multi‑threaded sum of tx counts. tx_sum = Ref{UInt64}(0) @sync begin for _ in 1:nthreads() @async begin for blk in chan atomic_add!(tx_sum, blk.tx_count) end end end end println("Parallel tx sum = $(tx_sum[])") ``` --- ## 5️⃣ Checklist for a production‑ready binding | Item | Why it matters | How to implement / verify | |------|----------------|---------------------------| | **Struct layout sanity** | Mismatched padding yields corrupted data. | Keep the `@repr(C)` Julia struct and the C++ `BlockInfo` in sync; call `blockinfo_size()` at module load (already done). | | **Thread‑safety** | Users may call `eachblock` from multiple Julia threads. | The C++ iterator is *not* thread‑safe; guard calls with a global `ReentrantLock` or document that `eachblock` must be called from a single thread. | | **Exception handling** | A user closure might throw; we must not crash C++. | C++ `walk_blocks` catches all exceptions and returns a non‑zero error code; Julia sees the original exception because the callback is invoked via `cfunction`, which propagates the exception back to Julia. | | **Memory safety** | The `BlockInfo*` is only valid for the duration of the callback. | Document this clearly; the shim does not store the pointer anywhere. | | **Performance profiling** | Ensure the hot path stays ~2 ns per block. | Benchmark with `@btime` on a synthetic loop (`walk_blocks` over 10 M blocks) and compare to a pure C++ loop. | | **Binary compatibility** | The shared library must be discoverable on all platforms. | Use `Libdl.dlopen` with a relative path; provide a `deps/build.jl` script that builds the C++ lib for the target OS (Linux/macOS/Windows). | | **Versioning** | The underlying Blocksci library may change. | Export a `blocksci_version()` symbol and expose it as `Blocksci.version()` so Julia code can guard against API changes. | | **Packaging** | Users expect a `Pkg.add`‑able package. | Wrap the C++ compilation in a `BinaryBuilder` artifact (or a `deps/build.jl` that falls back to a local `make`). | --- ## 6️⃣ TL;DR – What you copy‑paste 1. **`blocksci_bridge.cpp`** (the C++ file). 2. **`blocksci_bridge.h`** (the tiny header). 3. **`Blocksci.jl`** (the Julia module). Compile the C++ source into a shared library (`libblocksci_bridge.so` or `.dylib`) and put it next to `Blocksci.jl`. Then `using .Blocksci` gives you the two high‑level entry points: * `Blocksci.eachblock(f::Function)` – the fast callback style (recommended). * `Blocksci.BlockIterator()` – a lazy iterator for convenience. You now have a **Polars‑/pandas‑style per‑row API** for the massive BlockSci dataset, with the hot‑path staying fully in C++ and only a single, allocation‑free call into Julia for each block. Happy hacking!
